sentences.text: Yeah.
Right.
And in three weeks, I proved to Mengele that I was well.
Mengele, in my case, never, ever talked to me.
He talked about me.
He talked at me.
But you have to realize, Mengele did not treat us as human beings.
We were just his treasured experiment subject.
We were-- it's a very strange relationship, because in the barrack itself, when we-- where I went with the other twins-- he was very handsome.
And there were some girls who were 12 and 13 years old.
And they would have-- they would say, oh, he's like a movie star.
And they would have a crush on him.
In addition to that, all of these children have lost their whole families.
So he became the father image.
He became our father and our tormentor-- our protector and tormentor at the same time.
I would say that, in all of these bad things, there was still one pretty lucky thing-- that this monster of a doctor was interested in experiments on twins.
If he wouldn't have been interested in experiments, I wouldn't be here to tell you my story.
So in all that crazy, crazy miserable place, I have to look at it as still some-- some strange stroke of luck that I'll-- at least I had a little chance to survive-- not a great deal, but a little.
Once my fever was down, they released me.
I returned to the camp, and I was so, so delighted to see Miriam.
And she looked completely lifeless.
She was like one of these walking Muselmanns.
And I just left her in the camp, in the barrack.
She didn't say much.
But the biggest problem was, in the camp, that once a person lost the will to live, you were gone.
It didn't take very long to die.
Dying was very easy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So finally, I decided that I must try to organize some potatoes.

And the following morning, after roll call, when they would choose the person to go to the kitchen-- two of us would carry these 30 gallon containers filled with some kind of a liquid-- usually black liquid that they called coffee.
I volunteered.
And I jumped high enough.
I yelled loud enough that I was picked.
And I went to the kitchen.
And I remember that picture so clearly in my mind.

And any time I am embarking on something new in my life, that picture comes back to me. 'Cause I was sure-- the rumor was-- again, everything was by rumor-- that if I was caught, I would-- I would be hanged.
If anyone was caught stealing in Auschwitz-- would be hanged.
Well, I didn't know how to steal.
I didn't know how to organize.
I have never done it.
There was this long, long table with lots of pots, big pots, on it.
And underneath, there were a few sacks with potatoes.
And I hesitated for awhile.
And then I said, well, I have got to save her life.
Whatever she has, potatoes are the only thing that will help.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And I bent down, and I could feel in my hand three potatoes.
I could almost-- and then somebody pulled me up by my neck, and looked straight in my face-- a woman who was working there-- and said, it is not nice to steal.
And I almost started laughing.
I thought I was going to be marched out and hanged.
I realized then that to be a Mengele twin, only Mengele could touch us-- no one else.
And so next day, I came back.
And at this time, I was not afraid anymore.
And I became a very, very good organizer.
I-- we had potatoes three or four times a week.
We would boil them in little pots in the evening.
We had a little brigade of organizers that would stand guard so the supervisors wouldn't find us.
We organized coal.
And we would-- one would boil the potatoes, and the other one would use the same pot-- without any salt, with the skin.
And the steady diet of potatoes cured Miriam, or at least helped-- got-- got her a lot stronger.
She got rid of her dysentery.
And we didn't know at the time, but she suffered from severe kidney infection.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Toward the end of the summer, the gypsy camp was eliminated.
And we were taken into the gypsy camp.
And the rumor was, of course, that we would be next.
As far as the experiments are concerned, what I know about the experiment-- there were two distinct type of experiment that were done on me.
And there were some other experiments that were done on older children-- on teenagers.
In our case, part of the experiment was done in the blood lab.
The problem was not only was the blood taking that made us very weak, but with the shot, the injections, that were given into the arm-- that were germs of all sorts of kind.
There were all kind of other tests that they were running.
We know that the twins were used and were injected with all kind of chemicals and germs.
What they were, we do not know up to date.
The other part of the experiments were done in Auschwitz.
We would be marched there, put in a huge room, maybe 10 sets of twins at a time in one room with about 5 or 6 doctors all dressed in white coats.
They would keep us naked.
They would measure every part of our body, compare it.

Let's say, put an instrument and squeeze it against our skull to see the size of the head; then compare it to child's; then compare it between each set of twins-- the nose, the ears, the eyes, the shape of the face.
On occasion, they would photograph everything.
Other times, they would paint us with something that was like a dark-- some kind of a paint.
And then they would wipe it up and wash it off.
We were-- these experiments, for all its practical purposes, were not deadly.
But they were extremely difficult to deal with-- six to eight hours being naked.
I don't think that anybody, even us in Auschwitz, wanted to feel like a piece of meat.
And we have been reduced, in this case, to a mass of breathing, living cells.
So much of the detail of the experiment I still do not remember, because the only way I could cope with it is by blocking it out.
I would let my mind just take off into other directions.

I know from some of the twins, and from the testimony from the mock trial that I organized in Jerusalem, in 1985, that Mengele would take twins in reproductive age, which would be teenagers-- 12, 13, 14, 15-- and castrate some of the boys.
I have heard of one testimony.
Then, he-- and I talked to one of the twins myself, a 16-- she was a 16-year-old girl-- and did cross transfusion between boys and girls.
Most of these twins died.
Those who are alive, two of them live in Australia.
They have had severe health problems.
What he wanted to do, in this case, he wanted to figure out if he could change sex.
He wanted to have complete control.
If he wanted to create blue-eyed blondes, he could do that; if he wanted to create certain features in people; and if he wanted to create boys.
He wanted to push some genetic button that create boys.
He wanted to create girls.
He wanted to be able to create girls.
Yes, he has done some crazy experiments, which you could call pseudo-scientific.
We do not have any of the data.
We do not know now what the results were.
But he has done something very interesting.
He has injected one twin, like me, with a deadly germ.
Would I have died, Mengele would have killed immediately my twin sister with an injection of fennel to the heart, and do the comparative autopsies.
I compared notes with my late sister, Miriam, in 1985.
For the first time, we compared notes.
And she said, for the first two weeks, she was kept under SS surveillance.
They didn't tell her where I was.
She was not taken to the lab for any test.
And all she could say is that they were waiting for something to happen, which she didn't know what it was.
After the two weeks were up, would I have died, they wanted to take her immediately so there wouldn't be no big difference in the time of death.
Twins are nature's magic research material-- identical twins.
You cannot duplicate these experiments in real life.
You inject one twin with a germ.
Then, you do the autopsy, and you compare the two bodies.
And you can see how the germ, how the chemicals work.
I didn't die.
Miriam was taken back to the lab, and she was injected with something that destroyed her kidneys.
She was never taken to the hospital, because she was not supposed to die.
She suffered a lifelong problem of the kidney disease.
Obviously, whatever I was injected with, since it didn't kill me, it made me stronger.
Because I have been always healthier than she was.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I know that one of the twins that I talked to-- she's a Dutch twin.
She will not give any interviews.
She was used in some very bizarre experiments.
And it was an interesting story that my-- she called my sister for two years, from 1985 to 1987, and would tell her, I don't know how you do it.
How you guys go forward and you talk about it?
She says too-- but I want-- I want you to know that I am the Dutch twin.
That's all I want you to know.
She says someway-- someday I just want to be able to call you and talk to you.
Miriam said, OK.
In 1987, she finally came forward.
And I'll tell you an interesting little story.
I was-- at the time, I was in Israel, in Beilinson Hospital, donating my left kidney to my twin sister, because her kidneys failed.
And here comes Juppi, on two crutches, because braces on her legs.
It was September.
No-- it was November.
It was very hot in Israel-- a very hot November.
And she's coming.
And we were in the hospital.
And with the crutches, and in each crutch a huge Coca-Cola bottle-- she's entering.
I am Juppi.
Hi, Eva.
How are you doing?
I'm looking at her.
I said, Juppi, you're crazy.
And she said, yes.
And I said, well, I don't know this woman.
I must have hurt her feelings.
And I said oh, don't worry so much.
I said, I'm kind of nuts myself.
And she said, but I have a certificate to prove it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she said she was so many times in the hospital, she knows that they don't give you too many Coca-Colas in Israel.
And she-- the said, I thought that you would like some Coke.
But it was a very unique picture.
And she was very, very close to my sister-- very close.
I'm just trying to tell you, she told me, at that time-- she wouldn't let me tape her-- that her colon was connected to her urinary tract by Mengele.
She was on the operating table, and she could feel the body of her brother turning cold.
She does not remember who liberated her or how she survived.
She has had a lifelong health problem, as well as emotional problem.
She is part of 10 Dutch twins.
The other 9 will not come forward.
They cannot deal with any of it.
She is doing very well today.
And she's very grateful that we have formed the support group.
So from what I can tell you-- that there were some very bizarre experiments.
No.

It's a very interesting question, because children, or I, as a child, when you are faced with life and death, the only thing that we ever talked about is food, survival, and how to make it from one day to the other.
There was-- I never even learned the name of many of the twins in the barrack.
We did not have a [NON-ENGLISH] like the boys did, who would try to introduce one another so we would know that we are a group.
We did not have that.
So we were left to our own misery.
We did not get better food than the rest of the people, like the boys did, the boy twins did.
Because there was no one to stand up for us.
We were very alone.
There was never any talking.
The other thing is that even I and my sister never talked about it.
The first time I ever mentioned it to her was 1979.
And then she wasn't quite-- because I wanted to find out what she remembered.
I couldn't find anything in books.
And it dawned on me that we never, ever discussed it.
The issue never came up.
Now, I want to kind of understand it.
Let's say that somebody is in a very traumatic situation.
Maybe the first sign of severe trauma for a person is that they cannot verbalize their fear.
It's being petrified.
That's basically the best description to give.
You-- you-- you are just completely unable to relate to another human being and verbalize what you feel.
Maybe it's a defense mechanism.
I am not really sure.
That is for experts to discuss.
In late 1944, and I am not really sure it was November-- or it could be December.
One day we woke up, and it wasn't morning.
It was noon.
And the SS was gone.
And we were everywhere-- no SS.
So some of the-- that was after probably one month of transports being taken out of the camp.
We-- we could sense that the end was very near.
All we was trying to do is we were trying to survive the-- the end of that horror.
And some of the people were left behind-- came-- cut the barbed wire.
Then we went from one camp to the other.
I explored the whole camp.
I remember walking very close to the gas chamber and crematorium.
And I was very curious to go inside it, but I was sure that if I walked in, I would never walk out.
So I never went inside.
I did go to the Canada building and looked for some clothes, blankets.
I couldn't find any shoes.
My shoes were all worn out.
There were no children's shoes.
And then I went back.
And one day I went to the kitchen.
As I am in the kitchen with some other people-- we are organizing bread-- I heard this strange sound, the sound of a car.
I said, boy, they are back, or what's going on?
And I am going to the door.
And I had about five or six loaves of bread in my hand.
And I see a jeep with four SS.
And I see their machine guns, one of them pointing it straight at my head.
I woke up some time later.
And I was sure, I was absolutely sure, that I was in the other world.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I tried to move my legs.
They were moving.
And I said, well, I guess that's the way it is in the other world-- until I tried to move some people that were lying around me.
They couldn't move.
The only thing that I can say is that I definitely had a guardian angel that, before the bullet ever hit me or anything hit me, I fainted.
I ran back to the barrack, and I told Miriam what was happening.
And she said, well, don't worry so much.
We still have a little food.
And I am so grateful that you're alive.
We went to sleep.
We woke up in the middle of the night.
You could feel the heat coming through the ceilings, through the walls.
Your-- we looked outside-- the whole sky was red on fire.
The whole camp was on fire.
The same four SS with the jeep were waiting outside.
They blew up the remaining gas chambers, crematorium, Canada building, and some of the buildings with-- with as much evidence as they could destroy, and ordered us to march.
The sky was filled with fireworks.
I mean, we were the battlefield.
The allies were closing in, and they were fighting.
We had to move very fast.
If we didn't move fast enough, they will shoot.
If-- if you-- if you stayed behind, they would shot.
So finally, we are-- reached Auschwitz.
It was 1 o'clock at night.
And the four SS disappeared.
We raced for the buildings, because we didn't know if they were going to start shooting.
As we raced for the buildings, I got separated from Miriam.
And I got very scared.
[SOBBING] I walked 24 hours from building to building.
And luckily, I bumped into her in one doorway.
There are two sets of twins, two siblings, who had got separated in that same death march, and they still haven't found their sibling twin.
As I said, we were the battlefield.
We had to tend for ourselves.
We couldn't find too much food yet.
Most of it was gone.
I remember going down one day to the river, because we had no water to drink.
There was a river-- there is a river-- near Auschwitz camp.
And I lowered the little bottle tied to a string and broke the ice.
It was very cold.
And as I was pulling up the water, I was looking at the other bank of the river.
There was a little girl about my age, dressed in beautiful clothes, with the longs braids and ribbons in her hair, and a school bag on her back.
And I was absolutely shocked.
I could not believe that there was out there a world, where children were living like children.
And this was my first realization that there indeed was a world out there.
[SIGHS] She looked at me too.
And I could only imagine that what she thought about me.
I was dressed in dirty clothes, swarming with lice.
And the only school I attended was the school of survival.
About a few-- maybe 10 days later, on a snowy day, it was very quiet.
It was dead silent.
There was nothing going on.
It was suspicious.
I didn't dare even look outside.
Finally, by middle of day, somebody went to the door and said, we are free.
We are free.
So we all ran to the door.
And I tried to look.
I couldn't see anything.
I couldn't see anybody coming.
People were running to the right-- to the right.
I kept looking.
The snow was falling.
You could see almost nothing, until the people were almost in front of us-- the Soviet army.
They were all dressed in white raincoats with white hoods.
And people started kissing, and dancing, and-- and-- we are free.
We are free.
We are free.
That night, the Soviet soldiers came, brought candy, and brought vodka, and started dancing the [NON-ENGLISH] in the middle of that stupid barrack.
We stayed in Auschwitz.
And the following day, they took us out, put on us striped uniforms, and marched us between the barbed wire, and took pictures of us-- movie cameras.
And I thought, gosh.
Now that's interesting.
I guess we are movie stars, and what?
I didn't understand why they were so interested.
All I really wanted to do is go home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I am glad today that they did take those pictures.

We were taken from there to Katowice, to a monastery, where we were supposed to-- the rumor was, or maybe they told us-- that we would wait there until the war ended, and then we would be taken to Palestine.
And of course, I wanted to go to Palestine eventually, but I wanted to go home first.
The first night there, I remember very clearly, because we were given nice, small rooms with two beds, and a white sheet.
I have never, ever seen anything so white.
It was gleaming.
And I kept looking at that sheet.
And I couldn't sleep on it, because I felt dirty.
I was still filled with lice.
I still had the-- the-- the-- the clothes that I had in camp.
And I-- just going to-- I was going to get these sheets dirty, so I had to remove the sheet, and just sleep on the mattress.
And they gave us toys.
I remember dolls.
And I felt very, very insulted.
I realized that they have no idea that the child who survives life and death cannot play with dolls.
I-- I-- [SOBBING] I think the children maybe-- I don't know at what age it starts-- but I would say all children who are faced with life and death situations are no longer children.
So I just pushed the dolls inside, and I never wanted to look at them.
We stayed there for a few months.
And there were many things I didn't like about the monastery.
I didn't feel very comfortable in a Catholic monastery with-- they didn't try to convert us or anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Excuse me.
It was run by the Red Cross and by the Jewish Joint.
The nice thing was that we could leave any time and come back as we pleased.
We would show our numbers, and we would get on the street car, one that ran by, and travel the-- one end of town to the other.
And we did.
It was great fun.
And we found out somehow that, at one edge of town, there was a refugee camp.
And we wanted to find Mrs.
Csengeri.
Because I knew that she survived with her two twin daughters-- and wanted her to come and sign some papers, so we could go with her.
I could have walked away, but I didn't know where to go by myself in the big world.
I was just-- just-- tooks-- I wanted to go home.
That's all I wanted to do.
And so she came.
And she took care of us.
And we stayed with her all the way until we got home.
We were-- there-- people were grouped in these refugees camps, or DP camps-- were grouped according to country of origin.
We were in a camp in Katowice.
Then from there, we were taken to a camp in Czernowitz.
In Czernowitz, we thought boy, we are getting close to the Romanian border.
We were given fairly decent food.
We were given some army shirts.
And Mrs.
Csengeri made them into dresses for us.
And she burned the other dresses with the lice.
And she cleaned us of lice.
And we stayed there for about three months.
And then one day, they said, pack up.
And we got on the cattle cars.
These cattle cars were open, and it wasn't sealed or anything.
And we had bunk beds in it.
And it was spring, and the-- everything was blooming, and the birds were singing, and the train was moving very slowly.
Sometimes it would stop.
And we would make-- Mrs.
Csengeri would make-- to take two bricks and start a fire, and cook something.
And the train was going deep into the Soviet Union, which I didn't know.
Many people would jump off the train.
The railroad track went up high here.
And I remember seeing people jumping and rolling down.
I said, why are they doing that?
I couldn't understand it.
I didn't know anything about communism.
I didn't know that the Soviet Union was communist.
I'd never heard about it.
But I am gathering now that they probably realized that the train was going deep into the Soviet Union, and they were jumping the train.
